The surgeon performs an intrafascial dissection of Denonvilliers' fascia laterally, carefully avoiding cauterization or cutting near the artery. The prostate is lifted as the fascia is released on both sides, allowing for a full nerve preservation approach. The surgeon uses the XI system to facilitate the nerve-sparing technique from below, releasing nerve bundles without opening the endopelvic fascia initially.

The surgeon opens the endopelvic fascia at the base of the prostate, revealing the intact capsule and releasing the retrograde nerve bundles. Clips are placed while preserving the lateral prostatic fascia. The prostate is kept elevated to avoid clipping nerve bundles, especially in smaller prostates. The dorsal venous complex (DVC) dissection is discussed, with a focus on preserving or ligating it at the end of the procedure.

The surgeon performs a retrograde release of the neurovascular bundle without ligating the dorsal venous complex (DVC), preserving anterior nerve fibers near the prostate. The dissection reveals a pink fascia layer left intact, aiming to improve functional outcomes by sparing peripheral nerves.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the prostate, preserving a cushion of tissue to minimize bleeding. A quill suture is placed to control bleeding from deeper veins, with plans to restitch later. The apex of the prostate is exposed, and the catheter provides feedback during dissection.

The surgeon adjusts the margin around the prostate, leaving a couple of millimeters of tissue intact. Using sharp scissors, the surgeon performs precise dissection, ensuring the urethra is well differentiated from the nerve bundles posteriorly. The urethra appears robust and circumferential, with minimal devascularization, contributing to a stable surgical field.

The surgeon examines the surgical field, noting the preservation of nerve bundles within the prostatic fascia. A small vein is left intact on one side for cushioning. The surgeon discusses the absence of visible 'train tracks,' indicating successful nerve preservation. The urethra and apex appear well-preserved, with minimal bleeding observed. The surgeon uses a Bob suture for closure, avoiding cautery or clips to maintain athermal conditions.

The surgeon performs posterior reconstruction using the Rocco stitch technique, incorporating the suture into the posterior anastomosis. The first layer involves reconstructing the posterior plate by joining the cut edge of denoviasis, while the second layer brings the bladder down to the urethra, enhancing hemostasis and reducing leakage. The double-arm suture technique refines the posterior part of the anastomosis, ensuring a robust connection between the bladder neck and posterior urethra.
